item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love this one. Good balance of comedy and horror. I also like the emerging Bee SCP genre and hope to see more from the SCBee Foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: one of my favorite slightly one-off GOIs has got to be HiveFind, it's just such a strange sounding place

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My favorite part of this turned out to be Iteration 2 of Hamlet, specifically "Ghost? Ghost."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't know why, but this is probably the funniest SCP I've read

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Definitely really cool and funny, with a good touch of horror mixed in. Honestly, this could very well have an extended test log with all the possibilities

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Beeeee!
First SCP-3034, now this? Hippo how did you do this

item.title: After reading this, I feel like this quote is most appropriate:
item.content:encoded: FORTINBRAS: What happened?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's CSS code (which relies on a custom template) that I (the author) added:

The Code
– hide block
 [[module CSS]] @import url(/component:scp-pride/code/1); [[/module]]

I added it to all my articles as a way of expressing queer pride. It's intended as a non-diegetic element (as in, not in-universe).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can someone explain to me why the foundation logo is rainbow on the article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
DESCRIPTION: A MOV file containing the entirety of Bee Movie, an animated film by DreamWorks Animation.

I read this then just sat there going “Oohh no no no no no” +1

item.title: Re: Author Post
item.content:encoded: 1+ for meme proximity

item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
I almost never oppose these things, but I'd be remiss not to point out that this article already has a link to the (quite excellent) bees:

NOTE: At this point, further testing was discontinued on account of Researcher Xiao-jin's computer inexplicably filling with bees.

Also, while I'm entirely for linking my articles together more tightly, I/O-SAURON remains one of my favorite I/O-based puns (with I/O-SILVER coming in a close second). Probably because of how flat-out dumb it is. I'd be saddened to lose it.

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author partially approved edits

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

Firstly, I propose linking to SCP-2639. This SCP mentions that things containing it need to be "TEMPEST-certified", and SCP-2639 explains what this means.

Secondly, I propose linking to Bees, because this SCP is about bees, and the Bees tale is about bees too.

Thirdly, I propose linking to SCP-3089. They both use I/O-bots in either their discovery or their special containment procedures.

Editing this:

Any computers, buildings, or facilities containing SCP-3045 must be TEMPEST-certified.

To this:

Any computers, buildings, or facilities containing SCP-3045 must be TEMPEST-certified1.

Editing this:

Under no circumstances should SCP-3045 be used on any media file that directly references bees.

To this:

Under no circumstances should SCP-3045 be used on any media file that directly references bees.

Editing this:

On 16-02-2010, a Foundation-operated web-analysis bot (I/O-SAURON) flagged several large-scale online purchases made by a California startup company ("HiveFind") as suspicious.

To this:

On 16-02-2010, a Foundation-operated web-analysis bot (I/O-SILVER) flagged several large-scale online purchases made by a California startup company ("HiveFind") as suspicious.

Footnotes
1. A certification indicating that equipment and/or structures are insulated from 'leakage' of data via electromagnetic or acoustic emissions. For more information, personnel with appropriate clearance may consult documentation for SCP-2639.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
After i read what it did to The Bee movie i almost threw up because i am terrified of bees

what would it do to other movies with bee?!
Imagine if i was used on the candyman…. i am terrified at the thought

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
275 minutes of someone getting stuck by millions of bees, while bees enter his mouth when he screams.

I thought the article was humorous at first and was going to +1, but then I read the results of the Bee Movie and still +1'd. What a ride.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
NATO's standards are perfectly fine for electromagnetic emissions; why would the Foundation need to establish their own?

They might have increased standards for concerns over things like anomalous divination or thaumaturgy, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any computers, buildings, or facilities containing SCP-3045 must be TEMPEST-certified.

This kind of TEMPEST? I thought the Foundation used its own emissions security standards.

aw hell yeah

bzz bzz motherfuckers

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of those perfect sort of SCPs that's short, "sweet", and kicks you more and more as you read through it. I felt a bit uneasy reading through it (though that might just be the melissophobia) even before the last test log, and seeing that really sealed the deal for me.

This is one of my favourite articles now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Hamlet abbreviation was funny and accurate. Just the right mix of humor and tongue-in-cheek violence. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Goddamn, how did I never see this before? That final test makes it. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Under no circumstances should SCP-3045 be used on any media file that directly references bees.

I upvoted as soon as I read this, and the rest of the article didn't disappoint. Damned fine job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP Foundation's bizarre running non-arc about bees is one of the best parts of the 'universe'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was marvelous, +1! I chuckled at how few iterations it took to boil down Waiting for Godot ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: can't believe you would spoil Waiting for Godot for all your innocent, unwitting readers

item.title: Re: Random Thought
item.content:encoded: This actually could make a very interesting tale if done properly…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bees?

item.title: Random Thought
item.content:encoded:
What if someone put a document infected with SCP-732 through SCP-3045? We know from the Great Prank War that SCP-732 is sentient - and that it likes long stories. I'd imagine it wouldn't be pleased with SCP-3045 shortening them down.

Anyway - nice article, gave me a good laugh. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In other words, this is the ultimate tl;dr. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TBH, I couldn't help but laugh a little at some of the iterations of the Bee Movie as dark comedy.. up until that last one with the computer being full of bees.

Great blend of comedy and creepy! +1

item.title: Re: A nitpick...
item.content:encoded: Wow, yeah, thanks — I completely forgot/blanked on the fact that honey doesn't need to be refrigerated. Fixing that >_>

item.title: A nitpick...
item.content:encoded: I loved it! It was pretty cool. Anyway, my little nitpick is the fact that honey doesn't need to be refrigerated. At all. And bees, especially, would know this. You could literally leave a jar of honey open on the counter for 100+ years and (barring anything like bugs or dust that flew into it) still safely eat it. It's a natural antibiotic. There's no reason to refrigerate it at all, and it just gets under my skin. Other than that, though, I loved it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have no idea what that reference is, and only vaguely knew that "Bee Movie" was a real thing, and it was still good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
NOTE: At this point, further testing was discontinued on account of Researcher Xiao-jin's computer was bees.

I think you tried to write something else but I can't figure out why. That's okay, article is bees. I gave it an upvote. Upvote is also bees. Yay!

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
What if bees were made of smaller bees?

What if bees were made of BIGGER bees?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's this? A thread dangerously underpopulated by bees? A large influx of bees should put a stop to that!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, that's pretty much exactly what the addendum was meant to imply xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure that's the general idea here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What if the company is actually run by anomalously intelligent bees, and they control the computers by standing in formation under the QR readers? That would explain why there's so much honey. Though this might not fit since the dead bees they found weren't anomalous

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
Iteration 3:

TB

item.title: Re: +100
item.content:encoded:
Iteration 2:
TV is bees.
What if bees were media?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I have a new favorite version of Hamlet. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As for most of the others, I would like to imagine that sufficiently advanced technology can pull it off if necessary

True. But would, say, a working quantum computer built in the 1970's not also be anomalous?

The problem isn't so much that you need magic to explain what it does so much as what it's doing is likely not technologically feasible at the time of creation. Maybe we're at the stage where somrine can develop a smart enough program can condense written media in a non-Dada-esque way.

But we're likely not at the stage where somwone can cteate programs that can generate new content to go along with the condensed script.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hahaha, the jar of mouse bites was really funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: House M.D. Iteration 5

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Granted, the last point is the closest to what I expect for 'cannot be explained under conventional science' (which is how I define SCPs by). As for most of the others, I would like to imagine that sufficiently advanced technology can pull it off if necessary.

I mean, you can say that they are done in a manner that cannot happen normally, but the end result seems to be something not too displaced from what that can be plausible under normal circumstances.

But even bees manifesting in a computer is not what I would call a strong claim to justify being an SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It can rewrite entire movies, adding/removing/modifying scenes (changing what the actors do and say) and — in the last instance — even transformed an animated film into live action. On top of that, it literally filled a computer with bees.

You're more than welcome to your opinion, but I'm a bit bee-fuddled: You don't see the anomaly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I will play the devil's advocate here, and say that this rides practically on rewriting at various pre-existing media. While it is fitting to the article, that is majority of it and I do not truly see the anomaly amidst all this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article is unbeelievably good. +1'd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: really nice, really liked the twist of what happens when you put bee related things into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The later Hamlet iterations are the best thing I have seen today.

Take my +1

bzzz εїз

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this.
Well done.
1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is something else. Very well done. Not to detract from the effort you put into the piece elsewhere, but Iteration 6 of Indiana Jones really got the biggest reaction out of me. Again, well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked it before, still liking it now. Tab 5 of Hamlet really captures the spirit of the play.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely hilarious while being nice and disturbing. While obviously a reference to the "Bee Movie but every time X happens it Y's" you never relied on the reference itself for humor, making it your own thing. Bravo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am no longer moody. +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was seriously tempted to create an extended test-log where they started using it on SCP articles (ones that don't reference bees), but then I remembered SCP Explained exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funny as hell while still being unnerving. Basically my favorite kind of SCP. +1.
